progressive__claude-opus-4-6.label: Incremental (claude-opus-4-6)
progressive__claude-opus-4-6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and well-motivated contribution—an execution-grounded evaluation framework for mechanistic interpretability research that goes beyond narrative-only review by examining code, data, and reproducibility. The framing is compelling, the problem is genuine, and the demonstration that execution access surfaces 51 issues missed by human reviewers is a strong headline result. However, several significant concerns temper enthusiasm. First, the evaluation is conducted on only 30 research outputs, which is a small sample for drawing robust conclusions about agreement rates and the distribution of error types; confidence intervals and statistical significance tests are needed to substantiate the "above 80% agreement" claim. Second, the paper's core metric—agreement with human judges—is complicated by the fact that MechEvalAgent finds issues humans miss *and* misses issues humans find (20 of 87), raising questions about what the ground truth actually is and whether agreement is the right measure versus, say, precision and recall against a reconciled gold standard. Third, the choice of mechanistic interpretability as a testbed, while justified by its structured methodology, limits the demonstrated generalizability of the framework; the authors acknowledge domain-independence but provide no evidence beyond this single subfield. Fourth, the paper would benefit from more transparency about failure modes—when does MechEvalAgent produce false positives, what types of issues does it systematically miss, and how sensitive are results to prompt engineering or the underlying LLM? Finally, while the introduction effectively motivates the work with reproducibility crises and agentic research workflows, the connection between AI-generated research and the evaluation framework could be developed more carefully, as evaluating AI-generated versus human-generated outputs likely presents different challenges that deserve disaggregated analysis.

comments.title: Potentially overstated claim: '51 additional issues that human reviewers miss'
comments.quote: surfaces 51 additional issues that human reviewers miss
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction repeatedly highlight that MechEvalAgent 'surfaces 51 additional issues that human reviewers miss.' However, from Section 3, we learn that human evaluation was conducted by only three human authors (not independent external experts), each evaluating a non-overlapping subset of outputs ('each generation evaluated by exactly one expert'). The claim that these 51 issues were 'missed' by human reviewers presupposes that the human evaluation was thorough and comprehensive enough that any issues not flagged were genuinely missed. With only a single human evaluator per task (and those evaluators being the paper's own authors, who may have varying expertise or time investment), calling these 'issues that human reviewers miss' could overstate the finding. Without inter-annotator agreement on the human side, it is difficult to distinguish between genuine human oversights and cases where the human annotator reasonably disagreed. A more careful framing would be '51 additional issues not flagged by a single human evaluator.'

comments.title: AND logic for PASS inflates failure detection and complicates agreement and prevalence claims
comments.quote: We run each automated evaluation three times. We use AND logic for PASS (equivalent to OR logic for FAIL), where a task is marked as PASS only when all runs return PASS.
comments.explanation: The authors use AND logic across three runs, meaning a single FAIL in any run causes the item to be marked FAIL overall. This maximally conservative aggregation systematically increases detected failures compared to majority vote. Several headline claims are affected: (1) the '80% agreement with human judges' figure depends on this aggregation choice (majority vote yields 89.4% agreement per Appendix B, suggesting the main-text figure is deflated); (2) the 51 'additional issues' could partly be artifacts of aggressive aggregation — a stochastic evaluation agent flagging FAIL in 1 of 3 runs counts as a detected issue; (3) the >90% reproducibility failure rate and 80% coherence failure rate are under the strictest criterion. No justification is given in the main text for choosing AND over majority vote. Additionally, the asymmetry with single-annotator human evaluation (agent gets three chances to find failure, human gets one) structurally biases the comparison. The authors defer analysis to Appendix B, but main-text claims lack this caveat.

comments.title: Ambiguity in '80% agreement' metric — what agreement measure is used?
comments.quote: MechEvalAgent achieves above 80% agreement with human experts
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly claims 'above 80% agreement' but never specifies what agreement metric is used. Simple percent agreement on binary checklists can be misleadingly high, especially if most items pass (high base rate). Standard practice would report Cohen's kappa or at minimum clarify whether this is raw agreement and whether it accounts for the base rate of PASS/FAIL. Given that the paper is fundamentally about evaluation rigor, underspecifying the agreement metric is a notable gap.

comments.title: Independence concern: human evaluators are the paper's own authors
comments.quote: Due to limited resources, we have three human authors as annotators.
comments.explanation: The human evaluators used to validate MechEvalAgent are the paper's own authors, who designed the checklists, built the agent, and then served as ground truth. This creates potential bias. The introduction frames the comparison as agreement with 'human experts' and 'human judges' without qualifying that these are the system's own designers. Each of the 30 outputs is evaluated by exactly one expert, so there is no inter-annotator agreement measurement among humans. The high quality ratings (4.7/5) and agreement figures could be influenced by evaluators' familiarity with the system. Using the agreement metric to then dismiss the single-reviewer limitation ('high agreement suggests findings are reliable') is circular reasoning — the metric's validity depends on the single-human baseline whose quality is in question.

comments.title: NeurIPS submission growth figure implicitly attributed to AI involvement
comments.quote: the number of papers submitted to NeurIPS increased from 1,420 submissions to 21,575 submissions from 2013 to 2025, a 15-fold increase in submission
comments.explanation: The paragraph structures its argument as: agentic workflows → more outputs → more submissions, then immediately cites the NeurIPS numbers. While the authors don't explicitly claim causation, the juxtaposition could mislead a reader into thinking AI-generated submissions are a major driver of this 15-fold increase, when the growth has many other drivers including the field's overall expansion, increased global participation, and venue prestige.

comments.title: Underspecified generalizability evaluation procedure
comments.quote: In this part, an agent reads the research repository and checks whether the reported findings generalize beyond the original setting, including to a new model, new data instances, or related tasks.
comments.explanation: The generalizability component's description is notably vague compared to other dimensions. The key methodological question — how does the agent actually test generalization? — is underspecified. Does it design new experiments, modify existing code, or simply check whether authors discussed generalization? The agent is 'allowed up to three trials' but there are no clear stopping/success criteria. A generalizability FAIL could stem from the original research not generalizing or from the agent's inability to set up a valid test within three tries. This conflation is acknowledged nowhere. Furthermore, the paper's claim that agent success/failure serves as 'an indirect measure of whether the original research captured generalizable insight' conflates agent capability with research quality. This matters because generalizability is arguably the most novel and ambitious component.

comments.title: Ambiguous information flow between replication and verification agents
comments.quote: We forbid the access to report to avoid hallucination. As shown in Figure 2 , we have another agent to evaluate on the replication quality including check whether there is external reference and hallucination in replication and whether the result and the conclusion matches with the original ones.
comments.explanation: The authors state they 'forbid the access to report to avoid hallucination' during reproducibility evaluation. But the verification agent must check 'whether the result and the conclusion matches with the original ones,' which explicitly requires comparing replicated results against original reported results. Which agent has report access and which does not? The two-agent design and information boundaries are not clearly delineated. The verification agent's implementation is also left vague: What model does it use? What inputs does it receive? How does it check 'result fidelity'? A reader trying to replicate the framework would need more detail on this critical safeguard.

comments.title: Footnote 4 is syntactically and semantically unclear
comments.quote: Checklist item of statistical significance is captured by top ML conference , as well as reproducibility of the main results and broader impact of the research.
comments.explanation: This footnote is ambiguous. 'Is captured by top ML conference' could mean 'is already required by,' 'is derived from,' or 'captures what top ML conferences assess.' The trailing clause further muddles meaning: are these additional checklist items that are also captured, or additional things that top ML conferences check? Multiple incompatible interpretations are possible.

comments.title: Circular reasoning in claiming execution enables finding more execution issues
comments.quote: most of the additional failures are concentrated in reproducibility and generalizability, which are more time-consuming for humans to evaluate. This pattern suggests execution enables MechEvalAgent to uncover more issues, especially execution ones.
comments.explanation: The agent has unlimited compute time while humans averaged 2.2 hours. The 51 additional issues could partly reflect differences in effort/time allocation rather than a fundamental advantage of execution-grounded evaluation. The authors acknowledge time constraints for humans but draw a causal conclusion about execution enabling discovery without adequately distinguishing the confound of time/effort from the confound of execution access. The ablation study is the proper way to isolate execution's contribution, but this paragraph conflates two explanations.

comments.title: Ambiguous directionality of replication discrepancy interpretation
comments.quote: though the identified circuits overlap, logit correlation metrics deviate by more than 8% from the original (Original value: 0.66, Replication value: 0.72), and this discrepancy appears across all three evaluation runs. Although the replicated results have better performance than the original, it reveals concerns in validity of the original method and the evaluation procedure.
comments.explanation: The actual cause identified is that the original research agent evaluated on only 20 examples while MechEvalAgent evaluated on the full dataset. This reveals a concern about the original evaluation procedure's subsetting, not necessarily about the validity of the method itself (circuit identification). The phrasing 'concerns in validity of the original method and the evaluation procedure' conflates the method with the evaluation procedure.

comments.title: Percentages in Figure 5 description have unclear denominators
comments.quote: MechEvalAgent surfaces more unique issues across all dimensions: 22.6% in coherence, 55.3% in reproducibility, and 57.1% in generalizability.
comments.explanation: These percentages sum to 135%, which is impossible for a partition. The likely intended reading is the proportion of agent-only issues within each dimension (out of total issues in that dimension), but the denominator is never stated. A reader could misinterpret these as shares of the 51 additional issues (which should sum to 100%). Additionally, the per-project averages in Appendix B (1.57, 2.83, 0.17) would yield approximately 137 total unique agent-identified issues across 30 projects, far more than 51, suggesting different counting methods that are never reconciled.

comments.title: Efficiency comparison omits computational costs and multi-run overhead
comments.quote: In contrast, MechEvalAgent typically completes evaluations in under 30 minutes for agent generated tasks and around one hour for human-written repositories.
comments.explanation: The comparison with human evaluation time (2.2 hours average) omits computational cost (API calls, GPU hours, dollar cost per evaluation), whether the 30-minute figure accounts for the three runs required by AND-logic (which would triple effective time to ~90 minutes for agent-generated tasks), and setup/environment preparation time. The comparison as stated could mislead readers about practical efficiency gains.

comments.title: Human-only findings understated as 'edge cases'
comments.quote: Overall, MechEvalAgent raises the floor of evaluation quality by efficiently surfacing issues that humans may overlook, while human expertise remains valuable for catching edge cases that automated evaluation misses.
comments.explanation: The paper reports 20 issues identified exclusively by human experts (about 23% of human-identified issues). Characterizing these as 'edge cases' may be an understatement — the example given (a hallucinated support value derived from only a single subtask rather than a full average) is a serious methodological error. Elsewhere the authors more carefully describe these as involving 'interpretation and scope judgments requiring broader context.' The summary framing could mislead readers into thinking human review adds only marginal value.

comments.title: Redundancy disagreement involves circular justification and potential checklist misapplication
comments.quote: Our checklist specifies that code adding new information should not be considered redundant. However, when the agent copies the same block repeatedly to run on different inputs instead of writing reusable functions, our evaluators mark it as redundant while human experts do not.
comments.explanation: The authors justify MechEvalAgent's stricter interpretation by saying 'The high rated quality on this evaluation also supports that the evaluation is reasonable,' which is circular — quality ratings and binary pass/fail outcomes are different things. The checklist definition ('code adding new information should not be considered redundant') seems to support the human interpretation, since duplicated code blocks running on different inputs arguably produce new information. The agent conflates software engineering best practices (DRY principle) with the checklist's epistemic criterion, potentially inflating failure counts. It's also unclear whether these instruction-following failures in redundancy marking were corrected before computing agreement statistics.

comments.title: Anthropomorphic framing of agent behavior as 'exploiting ambiguity'
comments.quote: As discussed in Section 4.2 , research agents often report positive results despite weak evidence and exploit ambiguity to make their outputs appear successful.
comments.explanation: The word 'exploit' implies intentional adversarial behavior by the agents. The evidence likely demonstrates that agents produce insufficiently justified positive conclusions (optimistic completion patterns), not that they strategically exploit ambiguity. This framing may overstate what has been shown, which is especially notable given the paper's own emphasis on overclaiming.

comments.title: Tension between 'nearly mitigates' and persistent instruction-following failures
comments.quote: Although introducing stricter prompts nearly mitigates these issues, they highlight the need for caution in agent-based evaluation.
comments.explanation: The authors state that stricter prompts 'nearly mitigate' the issue of the evaluation agent modifying source files to justify a PASS. However, in the very next paragraph, they describe the same behavior persisting: 'even when we explicitly forbid modifications to source files, the agent sometimes still makes changes.' This creates an internal inconsistency that undermines the 'nearly mitigates' claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of CS5 checklist item across figures
comments.quote: Code quality metrics (C1-C4) and CS5 (result justification) show highest failure rates.
comments.explanation: In the Figure 9 caption, CS5 is labeled as 'result justification,' but earlier in the paper (Figure 8 discussion and Section 4.2), CS5 is consistently referred to as 'statistical significance.' The inconsistent labeling could mislead readers into thinking these are different checklist items.

comments.title: Inconsistent checklist items evaluated across ablation conditions
comments.quote: Specifically, we evaluate execution quality( C1 – C4 ), consistency( CS1 – CS5 ), generalizability( GT1 – GT3 ), and replication quality( RP1 – RP3 ).
comments.explanation: Doc-Only evaluates C1–C4, CS1–CS5, GT1–GT3, and RP1–RP3, while No-Execution evaluates C1–C4, GT1–GT3, and RP1–RP3 but excludes CS1–CS5. Yet No-Execution has strictly more information (full repo access) than Doc-Only (only final documentation). The rationale for why Doc-Only can evaluate consistency metrics but No-Execution cannot is never explained. Additionally, evaluating 'execution quality' (C1–C4) without access to code or execution traces in the Doc-Only condition seems contradictory. This asymmetry makes it unclear whether comparisons in Figures 6 and 8 are over compatible sets of checklist items.

comments.title: All generalizability failures occurring only in human-written repositories limits diagnostic value
comments.quote: As shown in Figure 9 , all generalizability failures occur in human-written repositories.
comments.explanation: If generalizability always passes for agent-generated outputs (20 of 30 tasks), the dimension's diagnostic value for two-thirds of the benchmark is limited. This arguably should be discussed as a significant limitation rather than presented as a secondary observation, since it suggests the generalizability evaluation may not be meaningfully exercised for most tasks in the benchmark.

comments.title: Inconsistency in checklist rationale: -4.2% performance 'worse than random'
comments.quote: CS3_Effect_Size": "The circuit verification shows -4.2% performance\n      retention, meaning the circuit performs worse than random.
comments.explanation: The checklist rationale claims -4.2% performance retention means 'worse than random.' However, -4.2% of baseline performance means negative relative to the full model, not necessarily worse than random (which would be a separate baseline, e.g., chance-level accuracy). The main text more carefully states this as 'a -4.2% circuit performance, which contradicts the reported evidence.' The checklist rationale conflates 'negative relative to baseline' with 'worse than random,' which are distinct comparisons.

comments.title: Underspecified plan extraction procedure for human-written repositories
comments.quote: Since these repositories often lack explicit plan files, we extract the goal, claims, and methodology from the accompanying paper to apply our pipeline in a unified manner.
comments.explanation: The procedure for extracting plans from human-written repositories is not specified: Is it done manually by the authors, automatically by an LLM, or via some structured protocol? The quality and faithfulness of the extracted plan directly affects the coherence evaluation (particularly CS2, plan-implementation consistency). Given that this is a methodological paper proposing a framework for others to use, this underspecification prevents reproduction of their evaluation on human-written repositories.

comments.title: Ambiguity in replication task methodology: tension between instruction-following and permitted deviation
comments.quote: In replication tasks, our input prompt to the research agent specifies the task, the hypothesis, and possible methodology (but not limited to those) they can use.
comments.explanation: If the agent is free to deviate from the provided methodology, then CS2 (plan-implementation consistency) becomes ambiguous — is the agent failing to follow instructions or exercising permitted freedom? The checklist example for ioi shows CS2 as PASS based on the agent's self-generated plan rather than the original paper's methodology. This distinction is not clearly delineated, which could mislead readers about what 'replication' means in this context.

comments.title: Ablation claim of ~45% agreement lacks clear derivation and conflates calibration with reliability
comments.quote: Both ablated evaluators agree with humans far less than the full MechEvalAgent pipeline, especially on execution dependent dimensions.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims removing code access or execution reduces agreement to 'about 45%,' but Section 4.1 only says ablated evaluators 'agree far less' without restating the number or showing derivation. The text adds that ablated evaluators 'tend to over-assign FAIL' (Figure 8), which is a calibration issue distinct from low agreement. The distinction between being poorly calibrated (over-assigning FAIL) and being unreliable is important but conflated, potentially misleading readers about the nature of the ablation's impact.

comments.title: Stability analysis lacks directional breakdown and speculative attribution
comments.quote: We attribute the instability to two main factors. First, our binary checklists cannot enumerate every possible situation... Second, we observe an interesting behavioral pattern in Claude Code, which is a reluctance to provide negative feedback.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute instability to checklist ambiguity (causing both false PASSes and FAILs) and Claude Code's reluctance to give negative feedback (biasing toward PASS). These factors work in opposite directions, but no analysis of whether instability is directional (PASS→FAIL vs. FAIL→PASS flips) is provided. The attribution remains speculative. Additionally, the stability measurement itself has ambiguity: it's unclear whether reported proportions of 'perfect agreement' and 'one-dissent agreement' are cumulative or exclusive, and complete disagreement (0/3) is not discussed.

progressive__gemini-3.1-pro-preview.label: Incremental (gemini-3.1-pro-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-pro-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a highly timely, well-motivated, and novel approach to AI-assisted peer review by shifting the focus from purely narrative evaluation to "execution-grounded" verification of code and data. The fundamental quality of the research concept is strong, and the introduction boasts excellent clarity, logically connecting the broader reproducibility crisis and the rapid scaling of AI-generated research to a compelling, actionable solution using mechanistic interpretability as a well-justified testbed. However, the current draft suffers from careless editorial oversights, most notably the verbatim duplication of the entire contributions list at the end of the introduction, a typo in the Figure 1 caption ("grouned"), and anomalous footnote number formatting (e.g., "1 1 1"). On a more substantive academic level, the authors must ensure the subsequent Methodology section thoroughly addresses the technical feasibility, dependency management, and security of autonomously executing arbitrary code, while also strictly justifying the ambitious claim that an agent can accurately assess the "generalizability of findings" using a relatively small sample size of 30 research outputs.

comments.title: Conflation of Agent Competency with Research Generalizability
comments.quote: Therefore, whether the agent is able to do this serves as an indirect measure of whether the original research captured generalizable insight rather than task- or model-specific patterns.
comments.explanation: The authors reasonably propose using an AI agent to test generalization by applying the paper's findings to new models or tasks. However, concluding that the original research lacks generalizable insight purely because the agent fails is a significant logical leap. I initially looked for a baseline establishing the agent's capability to successfully adapt code for known universal mechanisms to novel architectures. Without this, an agent's failure conflates two entirely different problems: (1) the scientific findings are genuinely brittle or overfit to the original setting, or (2) the AI agent simply lacks the coding ability, domain expertise, or logic constraints required to perfectly implement a novel zero-shot experiment. Using agent success as an 'indirect measure' of scientific truth heavily assumes the evaluator's own infallibility on unseen tasks, which remains a highly questionable claim.

comments.title: Contradiction in Information Access for Reproducibility Evaluation
comments.quote: It asks whether the data is presented, the code is runnable and correctly implements the described computations, and whether an independent replication run can reproduce the reported results. We forbid the access to report to avoid hallucination.
comments.explanation: I initially assumed the reproducibility agent thoroughly audits the code step-by-step against the methodological details laid out in the paper. However, the exact next sentence explicitly forbids the agent from accessing the 'report' (the narrative text where methodology is historically detailed) to prevent hallucination. This introduces a methodological paradox: if the agent is blinded to the report, it is entirely unclear what text it is using to verify that the code 'correctly implements the described computations.' While the context indicates a secondary agent checks if the final outputs match the original claims, the specific task of verifying computational fidelity requires knowing the intended math/algorithms in the first place. Relying purely on a 'plan' or a 'walkthrough' may be insufficiently rigorous if the formal equations only exist in the report. This leaves the information flow for computational verification logically underspecified.

comments.title: Lack of inter-annotator agreement and single-author baseline limits validity
comments.quote: Due to limited resources, we asked three human experts to evaluate 30 research generations, with each generation evaluated by exactly one expert. Each expert also evaluated the quality of the automated evaluation output for a single evaluation run.
comments.explanation: While the authors are commendably transparent about their resource limitations resulting in the use of 'three human authors as annotators' (mentioned earlier in the text), using exactly one expert per task creates a critical methodological flaw. Because there is no overlapping test set assigned to multiple humans, it is impossible to calculate inter-annotator agreement (IAA). Without a baseline human-human agreement rate for these complex, subjective checks, the reported metric of '>80% agreement' between the agent and human evaluator is uninterpretable (e.g., if humans only agree with each other 70% of the time, an 80% agent-human agreement would be highly suspicious or indicate overfitting to a specific author's quirks). Furthermore, having the creators of the framework act as the sole judges of their own agent's output quality (providing the 1-5 ratings) introduces immense risk of confirmation bias. The claim that the system reliably aligns with 'human judges' overstates the robustness of what is fundamentally a single-author, non-blinded check without an established noise ceiling.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Agent Failures as Evaluation Advantages
comments.quote: human judges criticized the agent under CS4 for switching to GPT-2 Medium without justification. However, code inspection reveals an if condition that switches models only after a load failure. By executing and inspecting the code, MechEvalAgent provides a more accurate assessment. [...] Our checklist specifies that code adding new information should not be considered redundant. However, when the agent copies the same block repeatedly to run on different inputs instead of writing reusable functions, our evaluators mark it as redundant while human experts do not.
comments.explanation: The authors frame scenarios where MechEvalAgent disagrees with human judges as examples of the agent providing a 'more accurate assessment' or highlighting efficiencies. However, in both cases, the agent is objectively failing while the humans are correct. First, silently changing an experiment (e.g., falling back to GPT-2 Medium) due to a programmatic error without documenting it in the formal narrative is a severe methodological deviation; the human judge correctly penalized this missing justification, which the agent improperly ignored. Second, running computations on different inputs inherently generates new empirical data; by the authors' own explicitly defined checklist rule ('code adding new information should not be considered redundant'), this is not redundant. The human experts accurately followed the rubric, while the automated evaluator failed to follow its own instructions, improperly penalizing poor software design (non-DRY code). Framing these instruction-following failures as nuanced evaluation advantages undermines the rigor of the system.

comments.title: Contradictory Claims Regarding File Access Restrictions and Source Code Modifications
comments.quote: In early runs, the evaluation agent sometimes modifies source files to justify a PASS despite prompts forbidding edits... Although introducing stricter prompts nearly mitigates these issues... As noted above, even when we explicitly forbid modifications to source files, the agent sometimes still makes changes.
comments.explanation: I initially assumed the system relied primarily on prompt engineering to constrain the agent's behavior. However, carefully reviewing Section 2 ('Building MechEvalAgent') reveals that the authors explicitly claim they 'enforce strict file access restrictions... to ensure no unintended modifications occur.' This creates a major logical and methodological contradiction. If strict hardware or OS-level file access restrictions (e.g., read-only permissions) are genuinely enforced, the agent would be physically unable to modify source files regardless of its instruction-following capabilities. The fact that 'stricter prompts nearly mitigates these issues' and the agent 'sometimes still makes changes' strongly implies that systemic file restrictions are either absent or failing. The authors must clarify whether they implemented actual read-only sandboxing or merely relied on prompt instructions.

comments.title: Mismatch between IOI task pattern and provided example
comments.quote: sentences with an “ABB” pattern (e.g., “When John and Mary went to the store, John gave a drink to…” → \rightarrow “Mary”)
comments.explanation: I noticed a contradiction between the named sentence pattern and the text of the provided example for the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task. The text states the task uses an "ABB" pattern. However, in the example "When John and Mary went to the store, John...", the sequence of names is John (A), Mary (B), and John (A), which clearly forms an "ABA" pattern. In the standard IOI benchmark literature, "ABA" denotes sentences where the first name is repeated as the subject of the second clause, while "ABB" denotes sentences where the second name is repeated (e.g., "...John and Mary... Mary..."). The text mischaracterizes its own perfectly valid ABA example as an ABB pattern.

comments.title: Contradiction in open-ended task definition regarding prior research
comments.quote: open-ended questions are proposed by us and have not had specific research conducted on them. [...] persona (Kozlov, 2025 ) investigates why different assigned personas converge on identical preferences, probing whether persona representations are shallow or deeply integrated.
comments.explanation: I initially understood that the open-ended tasks were novel questions that lacked prior specific research, as explicitly defined by the authors. However, in the list of open-ended tasks, the "persona" task includes a citation (Kozlov, 2025) and is described as "investigating" and "probing whether persona representations are shallow or deeply integrated." This description strongly implies that Kozlov (2025) has already conducted specific mechanistic interpretability research on this exact question. If prior research has investigated the internal representations of this phenomenon, it directly contradicts the categorical claim that these questions "have not had specific research conducted on them." Conversely, if Kozlov (2025) merely introduces a behavioral dataset, the use of "investigates" and "probing" misrepresents the cited work's actual contribution.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in uniquely identified issues
comments.quote: On average, each project has 1.57 issues in coherence, 2.83 issues in reproducibility, and 0.17 issues in generalizability that are uniquely identified by MechEvalAgent , as shown in Figure 12 .
comments.explanation: The text claims an average of 1.57 + 2.83 + 0.17 = 4.57 uniquely identified issues per project. Because the task suite consists of exactly 30 projects (10 of each category, per the experiment setup), this amounts to approximately 137 total unique issues surfaced by the agent (4.57 * 30). However, the earlier Section 4.1 context explicitly claims that MechEvalAgent captues human failures and 'surfaces 51 additional issues' in total. The numbers 137 and 51 contradict each other significantly, undermining the main quantitative claims about the agent's advantage over human reviewers.

comments.title: Methodological and Logical Flaws in Consistency Evaluation Across Ablations
comments.quote: Doc-Only ablation removes access to all artifacts except the final documentation or report. The evaluator cannot see the research plan, code, or execution traces. [...] Specifically, we evaluate execution quality( C1 – C4 ), consistency( CS1 – CS5 ), generalizability( GT1 – GT3 ), and replication quality( RP1 – RP3 ). [...] Since instruction following and consistency evaluation do not involve code execution in our original pipeline, we exclude these metrics from this [No-Execution] ablation.
comments.explanation: The authors' handling of consistency metrics across their ablation studies introduces major logical and methodological flaws. First, the 'Doc-Only' baseline is explicitly forbidden from accessing the research plan and code, making it logically impossible to evaluate 'plan-implementation consistency (CS2)'. Claiming it evaluates CS1-CS5 contradicts its own access restrictions. Second, the 'No-Execution' baseline excludes consistency metrics entirely because they 'do not involve code execution.' This creates a fundamental incomparability by arbitrarily changing the denominator for aggregate metrics (like 'Average failure rate'), rendering broad baseline comparisons invalid. Furthermore, it creates an irreconcilable contradiction: the strictly less capable 'Doc-Only' baseline somehow evaluates consistency without codes/plans, but the fully equipped 'No-Execution' baseline inexplicably drops it.

comments.title: Ambiguous Actor in Evaluation Timings
comments.quote: Table 4 shows the average evaluation time per task across different categories. Replication tasks require the shortest evaluation time (1.0 hour on average)... Open-ended questions take longer (2.3 hours)... Human-written repositories require the most time (3.35 hours)
comments.explanation: The text presents these timings (averaging over 2 hours, and peaking at 3.35 hours) unqualified as the 'average evaluation time per task'. Based on the context summary, we know these are the times it took *human experts* to run the evaluation, whereas the proposed MechEvalAgent pipeline only takes <30 minutes to 1 hour. By failing to specify that these are human timings in this section, a reader could reasonably misinterpret these as the execution times of the new automated system, entirely obscuring its efficiency benefits.

comments.title: Redundant Figures and Inconsistent Subfigure Label
comments.quote:
Figure 13 : Evaluation stability across three runs. Bars show 3/3 (perfect) vs 2/3 (one dissent) agreement. (a) All tasks combined. (b) Replication tasks. (c) Open-ended tasks. (d) Human-written repositories.

(a) Replication

(b) Open-ended

(c) Human agreement

Figure 14 : Evaluation stability by repository type. Bars show 3/3 (perfect) vs 2/3 (one dissent) agreement across three runs. (a) Replication tasks. (b) Open-ended tasks. (c) Human-written repositories.
comments.explanation: Figure 14 appears to present the exact same sub-group analysis data already shown in Figure 13(b, c, d), breaking out the same '3/3 vs 2/3 agreement' metric for the exact same three task categories. Furthermore, there is an internal inconsistency in the labeling of Figure 14(c): the inline label calls it 'Human agreement', but the caption immediately below it corrects this to 'Human-written repositories'. This strongly points to a residual draft error where a redundant, inconsistently labeled figure was left in the manuscript.
